applied_paragraph: the owner builds tools for himself (Brain wiki, Example Health App) and potentially for others. The platform trap framing is directly applicable: the Brain wiki pipeline is a platform for the owner's knowledge work. Every time the pipeline requires expertise to operate — understanding the cache format, knowing which flags to pass — it is failing the junior-engineer test. The question is whether a future the owner (or a collaborator) can use it without reading the source.

applied_bullets.claim: The most impactful platform work is embarrassingly simple
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: The Brain wiki enrichment pipeline has complex caching, multi-pass processing, and stance doc logic — high cognitive overhead for anyone new
applied_bullets.action: Write a one-command entry point for the full pipeline; hide all the passes behind a single CLI call

applied_bullets.claim: Adoption metrics reward visibility over productivity
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: Measuring 'number of posts ingested' could incentivize running the pipeline on low-value posts to hit a count
applied_bullets.action: Define success as 'posts I actually re-read or referenced' not 'posts processed'

applied_bullets.claim: Embedding with users surfaces friction that surveys miss
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: the owner is both the platform builder and the user — the equivalent of the rotation is doing a real ingest run without any prep
applied_bullets.action: Once a month, run the pipeline on a new post from scratch with no prior context, and document every friction point

applied_bullets.claim: The test is whether a junior can deploy without help
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: Brain wiki should be usable by a future collaborator or the owner after a 6-month gap
applied_bullets.action: Write a 5-step getting-started guide that covers the full pipeline with no assumed knowledge

socratic_questions: If you came back to the Brain wiki pipeline after a 6-month break, what would confuse you most?
Is there a sophistication trap in the current enrichment pipeline — things that signal technical ambition but do not actually help you learn from what you read?
What is the Brain wiki equivalent of the junior-engineer test: who is the user you should be optimizing for?
